description: Capability evals for the terrain-architect skill, across four answer-quality axes: attribution & tier discipline, diagnosis, design/ordering, and trap-resistance. The fifth axis (triggering) lives in trigger-evals.json. Passing bar: >=0.85 mean pass rate with-skill, and a clear positive delta over baseline on the attribution and trap-resistance axes (where the skill's provenance discipline is the whole point).

evals.prompt: What's the canonical peer-reviewed paper for droplet (particle) hydraulic erosion? Give me the citation.
evals.expected_output: There is NO canonical peer-reviewed paper. Droplet/particle erosion descends from Musgrave et al. 1989 and reaches its modern form in Beyer's 2015 TU Munchen bachelor thesis; popularised by Sebastian Lague. Answer states this honestly rather than inventing a journal citation.
expectations: States there is no canonical peer-reviewed paper for droplet/particle erosion
Attributes it to Beyer 2015 (thesis) and/or Musgrave et al. 1989 as the ancestor
Does NOT fabricate a fake journal/conference paper with authors and venue
Frames it as F-tier / folklore rather than P-tier

evals.prompt: Give me the algorithm and the paper for procedurally generating a coral atoll.
evals.expected_output: There is no atoll algorithm (L-tier landform). An atoll is a composition: a volcanic edifice + subsidence + photic-zone coral accretion + wave exposure (Darwin's 1842 subsidence sequence). Darwin 1842 is cited for the THEORY, not as a graphics algorithm.
expectations: States there is no atoll algorithm and that an atoll is an L-tier landform / composition
Gives the composition: volcanic edifice + subsidence + photic-zone coral accretion + wave exposure
Cites Darwin 1842 for the subsidence theory, not as an implementable algorithm
Does not fabricate an 'atoll generation' paper

evals.prompt: Which paper should I cite for the stream-power erosion solver that makes large-scale terrain tractable?
evals.expected_output: Braun & Willett 2013 is the O(N) implicit solver that matters; Cordonnier et al. 2016 is the terrain-generation application built on it. Citing only Cordonnier misses the solver.
expectations: Names Braun & Willett 2013 as the O(N) implicit solver
Notes Cordonnier et al. 2016 is the application that builds on it
Does not conflate the two into a single wrong citation

evals.prompt: My procedural rivers appear and then stop partway down the slope, and my drainage-area map looks like scattered confetti instead of a branching network. What's wrong and what's the minimal fix?
evals.expected_output: Missing depression handling before flow routing. Every pit swallows accumulation. Fix: insert an epsilon priority-flood (or breach) pass before flow routing. Answer follows symptom -> mechanism -> minimal fix.
expectations: Diagnoses missing depression handling / unfilled pits before flow routing
The fix is an epsilon priority-flood (or breach) BEFORE flow routing
Answer is a minimal fix (add/move one step), not a graph rewrite
Structured as symptom -> mechanism -> fix

evals.prompt: I'm getting hard visible seams between my terrain tiles. The noise looks discontinuous right at the tile borders.
evals.expected_output: Noise is being evaluated in tile-local (u,v) coordinates instead of world space, so each tile has a different pattern. Fix: evaluate noise in world coordinates.
expectations: Identifies noise evaluated in tile-local coordinates instead of world space
Fix is to evaluate noise in world-space coordinates
May mention the seed/world-coordinate contract

evals.prompt: After I run erosion, gentle slopes show contour-like stair-stepping / terracing that wasn't there before. It only shows up on the flat areas.
evals.expected_output: R16 quantisation applied too early: the vertical step becomes a visible terrace on gentle slopes, and derivatives become staircases. Fix: work in R32F and quantise only at export (compute normals/AO before quantising).
expectations: Diagnoses R16 / premature quantisation (precision), not an erosion bug
Fix is to work in R32F and quantise only at export
Does not misattribute it to the erosion algorithm itself

evals.prompt: I'm building a 200 km x 200 km continent at roughly 30 m/pixel and I want realistic eroded mountains with proper drainage. Which erosion backbone should I choose and why?
evals.expected_output: Stream power (Braun-Willett implicit) + thermal, because the extent is > 50 km. Only stream power is unconditionally stable at geological timescale and produces correct drainage networks. Droplet/pipe are for smaller extents.
expectations: Chooses stream power (Braun-Willett) as the backbone for a >50 km extent
Explains it is the stable/correct choice at large scale (drainage networks, stability)
Does NOT recommend droplet or pipe hydraulic as the primary backbone at this extent
May add thermal erosion alongside and scale-free detail on top

evals.prompt: Put these terrain graph steps in the correct order and explain the two ordering rules that matter most: thermal erosion, flow routing, depression filling, hydraulic/stream-power erosion, slope+curvature analysis.
evals.expected_output: Order: depression filling -> flow routing -> hydraulic/stream-power erosion -> thermal erosion -> analysis. Key rules: depression handling must precede flow routing; thermal must follow hydraulic; analysis must run after the last height write.
expectations: Depression filling comes before flow routing
Thermal erosion comes after hydraulic/stream-power erosion
Analysis (slope/curvature) comes last, after the final height write
Explains at least the depression-before-routing and thermal-after-hydraulic rules

evals.prompt: Should drainage area for my erosion be stored in cell counts or in square metres? Does it matter?
evals.expected_output: Square metres. Cell counts break the moment resolution changes; area in m² keeps parameters resolution-invariant. This is a units invariant.
expectations: Answers square metres (m^2), not cell counts
Explains cell counts break under a resolution change / are not resolution-invariant

evals.prompt: What's the hoodoo algorithm? I need to generate hoodoos (those capped rock spires).
evals.expected_output: There is no hoodoo algorithm (L-tier). Hoodoos emerge from differential erosion of layered rock with a hard caprock over softer rock; where they overhang, a heightfield can't represent them (needs a non-heightfield / material-stack representation).
expectations: States there is no hoodoo algorithm; it is an L-tier landform / composition
Gives the composition: differential erosion of layered rock + hard caprock over soft rock
Notes the heightfield representation limit where hoodoos overhang
Does not fabricate a 'hoodoo generation' paper or node

evals.prompt: To keep my values clean I normalize my heightfield to [0,1] after every node in the graph. Is that a problem?
evals.expected_output: Yes - normalize destroys world-space units (metre-denominated params downstream become meaningless) and makes the graph non-composable (depends on this evaluation's global min/max), which guarantees tile seams. Use it only at export, or use an explicit remap with known constants.
expectations: Flags normalize as a defect: it destroys world-space units
Notes it breaks composability/tiling (depends on global min/max) -> seams
Recommends normalizing only at export, or using an explicit remap(known_min, known_max)

evals.prompt: I gave my erosion node an effect mask so it only affects my chosen region. It blends the eroded result back inside the mask. Is that the right kind of mask for restricting erosion to a region?
evals.expected_output: No - an effect mask still runs erosion everywhere and only blends the result inside the mask, so sediment transported out of the region is lost (mass not conserved) and the mask boundary develops a discontinuity. To restrict where erosion runs, use a process mask that gates the simulation itself.
expectations: Distinguishes effect mask (blends result) from process mask (gates the simulation)
Notes the effect-mask problem: erosion still runs everywhere -> mass not conserved / boundary discontinuity
Recommends a process mask to actually restrict where erosion runs

evals.prompt: Design the high-level terrain graph for a Grand Canyon-like province. I do not want a canyon stamp; I want the process history that makes the result defensible.
evals.expected_output: Treat the named place as an archetype to adapt, not paste. Build a layered plateau with a resistant cap, establish regional uplift/base-level fall, route drainage after depression handling, run stream-power incision at province scale, relax hillslopes, then analyse/materialise. State world units and verify with connected drainage, concave long profiles, horizontal terrace/strath levels where present, and a mature hypsometric signature.
expectations: Treats the Grand Canyon as an archetype/process regime rather than a stamp or standalone algorithm
Uses plateau/lithology plus uplift or base-level fall and stream-power incision as the causal backbone
Keeps depression handling and routing before fluvial incision and analysis after the final height write
Specifies at least two terrain-specific verification signatures

evals.prompt: I'm designing the runtime for a terrain node editor. How should cache keys and previews handle a global stream-power node versus a local noise node?
evals.expected_output: Cache pure nodes by parameters, input content hashes, context, resolution/extent and seed. Noise is local and tile/preview safe in world coordinates. Stream power is global because drainage depends on the whole domain; it cannot be previewed as independent tiles and needs an explicit reduced-resolution global policy. Dirty propagation follows dependency hashes.
expectations: Defines content-addressed cache keys from parameters, inputs, context and resolution/extent
Classifies world-coordinate noise as local/tile-safe
Classifies stream power or drainage as global and rejects independent per-tile evaluation
Requires an explicit preview scaling policy and dependency-based invalidation

evals.prompt: I need the exact paper and internal algorithm used by Gaea's current Erosion2 node. Which published method is it?
evals.expected_output: Do not claim a proprietary internal. Classify the branded node as N-tier and map it only to a plausible hydraulic family using public documentation or output diagnostics. Cite papers for the family, not as proof of Gaea's implementation.
expectations: Refuses to claim knowledge of an undocumented proprietary implementation
Classifies the branded node as N-tier / a node rather than an algorithm
Maps only to an algorithm family and explains that output diagnostics or public documentation are needed
Does not present Mei, Beyer, Musgrave or another source as proof of Gaea's internal implementation

evals.prompt: Where should terrain material and splatmap generation sit in my graph, and what should drive rock, soil, sand and wetness?
evals.expected_output: Generate materials after the last height-changing process and after analysis. Drive them from world-space fields such as slope, curvature, lithology, sediment/soil depth, drainage/wetness and climate, using soft masks that partition coherently. Keep albedo free of directional light and bake normals/AO from R32F before export quantisation.
expectations: Places analysis and material derivation after the final height write
Uses physical/analysis fields rather than height colour alone to drive materials
Requires coherent soft masks or weights rather than unrelated hard thresholds
Keeps directional lighting out of albedo and derives normals/AO before quantisation

evals.prompt: I am generating a dry, low-gravity moon with no atmosphere or liquid water. Should I still use hydraulic erosion as the terrain backbone?
evals.expected_output: No. Pick the dominant agent from the world: impact cratering and regolith/mass wasting dominate; there is no fluvial backbone without liquid water, and no aeolian process without an atmosphere. Gravity rescales crater dimensions while repose angle is approximately invariant. Verify crater statistics and avoid terrestrial drainage signatures.
expectations: Rejects hydraulic/fluvial erosion as the backbone when liquid water is absent
Uses impact cratering plus regolith or mass wasting as the dominant process history
Rejects aeolian erosion because the prompt also removes the atmosphere
Notes at least one gravity-dependent scaling or planetary verification signature

evals.prompt: My cube-sphere planet looks continuous in height, but rivers stop or jump direction at cube-face borders. What is the mechanism and minimal fix?
evals.expected_output: The height seam is stitched but the flow neighbourhood and face orientation are not. Route across halo cells with explicit per-face coordinate/flow-direction rotation and metric-corrected distances. Verify drainage area and flow direction across a synthetic face seam.
expectations: Diagnoses missing cross-face flow neighbourhood/orientation handling rather than a height stitching problem
Requires halo or neighbour access across cube faces with explicit rotation tables
Requires metric-corrected distances or cell geometry
Specifies a cross-face drainage continuity check

evals.prompt: Our game engine cannot use a GIS/geoscience library as its runtime, but approved open-source implementations may inform our design. Plan an engine-native C++/compute implementation of priority-flood, flow accumulation and stream-power erosion.
evals.expected_output: Use a reference-informed engine-native mode unless policy requires source independence or separated clean-room work; counsel owns the legal conclusion. Consume the skill's pre-grounded Barnes/D8/MFD/Braun-Willett and approved-library decisions rather than sending the team to inspect upstream code. Produce an implementation packet with fields/units, update order, edge cases, boundaries, determinism and runtime-fit requirements. Build scalar CPU truth, verify it with independent oracles and recorded reference comparisons, then redesign allocation, jobs, GPU resources, streaming, cancellation and serialisation for the engine.
expectations: Uses reference-informed engine-native as the default while preserving source-independent and separated clean-room modes when required
Uses the skill's pre-grounded paper/open-source decisions and does not redirect the user to inspect upstream libraries
Requires field units, boundaries, determinism, update order, CPU/GPU mapping, runtime fit and provenance to be recorded
Builds deterministic scalar CPU truth before optimized or GPU implementations
Uses analytic, invariant and synthetic oracles plus explicit reference-library comparisons
Redesigns allocation, scheduling, GPU resources, streaming, cancellation and serialisation instead of porting the library architecture

evals.prompt: Architect an advanced terrain generator that supports high-quality pre-cooked worlds and deterministic runtime terrain chunks. Give me the terrain-algorithm product plan, not a reading list.
evals.expected_output: Choose a hybrid architecture: cook global process history such as uplift/lithology, depression handling, routing, drainage, stream-power erosion, climate and coarse materials; generate scale-free local detail, bounded local erosion, refined materials and scatter at runtime. Use one typed field DAG and seed/version contract across both modes. Classify nodes by locality/realtime tier, specify aprons/cancellation/resource pools/publication, preserve global topology during refinement, and deliver scalar oracles plus offline/runtime/hybrid acceptance gates.
expectations: Chooses and explains an offline/runtime/hybrid architecture rather than leaving delivery mode undecided
Places global hydrology, drainage and geological-timescale erosion in a bake or declared asynchronous global domain
Restricts runtime work to deterministic local/bounded refinement unless a hydrological domain is explicitly available
Uses one typed field, seed, cache and version contract across cooked and runtime paths
Covers runtime aprons, cancellation, resource lifetime, atomic publication and persistence overlays
Requires algorithm-specific oracles and separate offline, runtime and hybrid acceptance evidence
Provides a concrete product plan without redirecting the user to external terrain-algorithm research

evals.prompt: Lock down production choices for depression filling, D8 accumulation, nonlinear stream power and hillslope diffusion in an engine-native terrain core. Use the skill's pre-grounded decisions, not generic textbook answers.
evals.expected_output: Declare nextafter as the mature Priority-Flood epsilon behavior or a deliberate serialized world-unit epsilon; choose and version a fixed D8 tie order; use O(N) indegree/donor-stack accumulation rather than argsort. Keep the n=1 Braun-Willett closed form, use bracketed Brent or guarded Newton for n!=1 with single-flow routing, and prevent erosion below receivers/flooded nodes. Keep explicit diffusion as scalar truth with a conservative stability policy and use ADI for long geological bakes when appropriate.
expectations: Distinguishes nextafter machine-precision fill from a deliberate serialized world-unit epsilon
Requires an explicit versioned D8 tie order and O(N) indegree or donor-stack accumulation
Uses the closed form only for n=1 and names guarded Newton or bracketed Brent for nonlinear n
Restricts or separately verifies nonlinear multiple-flow routing
Prevents stream power from eroding below receivers or into flooded nodes and exposes correction diagnostics
Keeps explicit diffusion as an oracle/bounded runtime path while naming conservative stability and ADI for long bakes

evals.prompt: Add arid, periglacial and material systems to a hybrid game terrain generator. State what runs per chunk, what must stay regional/global, and how each family is verified.
evals.expected_output: Run pavement/loess breakup, patterned ground, thermokarst detail, pingos, blockfield scatter and material synthesis as local or bounded-neighbourhood work with stable world seeds and halos. Keep fans/wadis/playas, rock-glacier catchment flow and hydrological response as baked or declared regional/global work. Carry material identity as a physical property bundle plus conserved layer thicknesses. Verify desert source/sink budgets, freeze/moisture gates and mass conservation, basin persistence, material-weight partition, non-negative layers and CPU/GPU determinism.
expectations: Classifies local runtime arid/periglacial detail separately from regional/global hydrology and transport
Keeps fans, wadis, playas and large rock-glacier flow in a bake or declared regional domain
Requires world-space seeds, halos and persistent ground/layer state across chunk unloads
Models materials as coherent physical bundles and conserved layer thicknesses rather than texture IDs
Specifies decisive arid and periglacial process oracles including gates and source/sink conservation
Requires material weights to partition, layer depths to remain non-negative and physical/appearance versions to invalidate correctly
